column1: Thread Content
Any issues sowing winter wheat after winter oats? Not as good as a broadleaf break crop I know. There hasn’t been Wheat here for a long time.
No issues at all, they can be a good break crop.

I find it a good bit hungrier, and slower to develop, I like to put on some slurry pre sowing, or go early with some fert, if dry enough a bag of 10/10/20 shortly after the fert season opens, or go with slightly more in your first split in early March.

I think the alleopathy effect of the oats can slow down the wheat initially but then it gets going. I nearly feel that oats is a better break crop for winter barley than for winter wheat (no real evidence though).
I wouldn't disagree on the winter barley, but I think that is partly because fert tends to be put on earlier on w.b. and think very early fert, or slurry can decrease the difference.
I have been offered pig slurry if I want. What rate per acre should I go pre ploughing?

What are the indices like, I don't like going much over 2k to the acre and don't have some young lad or auld lad overlapping it, you hear of lads going 3k and more but don't think I'd like that myself.

As above, pig slurry is very rich and you dont want it overlapped, I will be going with slurry off a dairyfarm at about 3k, but it would not be in the same league as pig slurry, especially off a fatten unit.

Just ordering seed, first time growing winter wheat, what kind of seed rate do you go with on the dawsum the last few years, obviously depends on sowing date and TGW. Hoping to sow mid/late october

The TGW of Dawsum at our Dunleer seed plant is about 51g. I’d be aiming to sow 330 seeds/m2 in good conditions in early/mid October. I would have been lower in years gone by, however I’ve never had a cereal crop where I’ve said “I wish I had less plants” but I’ve certainly had crops where I’ve said “I wish I had more plants “… At a TGW of 51g, I’d be sowing 68kg/acre in early-mid October. If it’s late October and good conditions, I’d raise it to 355-360 seeds/m2. It’s likely that slug pressure will be high this year as it’s been so wet. Any plants of WOSR that I missed while discing are sparkling with slug slime.
Would you leave slug pelleting until the wheat emerges or after sowing ?

It would depend on a couple of things, If you have cultivated the WOSR stubble lightly during dry weather and there are very few if any volunteers left then that should reduce the slug numbers from the start. What I would do is go out at dark with a light and see how much slug activity is there, if there’s a lot then I’d be tempted to spread pellets a week before you plough. The easiest time to kill a slug or rat with poison is when they’ve nothing else to eat. If none of the above have been carried out before planting then I’d spread once the crop is sown and rolled. There haven’t been many winter cereal crops rolled in recent years. All of the above can apply to cereals after beans also. Some mightn’t agree but this is based on my experience of what has and hasn’t worked over the years. Note: if you’re out at night with a light looking for slugs, your neighbours may think you’re weird. I’ve crossed that line with some years.
I will be going with a fair share of Dawsum after beans.  Seemed to do well in general and performed extremely well in some dodgy fields that were surrounded by trees .

midlands,,, we waited until october last year, but shur it rained nearly all of october ended up sowing november. so we were thinking of going in late sept.
Sowing in September is a Septoria breeding program, you will need a good T0. There are no T0s that I would even consider fair to average let alone good.

As @gone says, you will be loading on the Septoria pressure by going before October. That said, there’s a fair yield penalty if you have pieces of fields missing if you get caught by weather. Be prepared to go in hard after Septoria with your T1 & T2, no watery cheapo mixes. Some people do plant wheat before barley if the wheat is following a break crop in order to lessen the impact of Take All on the barley.

Under the right conditions old varieties of wheat would survive ten days under water but that was in ploughing after beet . Show pony five tonne varieties would die in a damp shed .

Speaking from experience of water logging, I would consider wheat to have at least 3 times the toughness of barley in wet conditions. It doesn’t matter if the barley is 2 row, 6 row or the newest much advertised hybrid, wheat is the man, barley is a child compared to it. In terms of survival, if seeds have germinated before the seedbed gets really wet then they have a much greater chance of surviving than if they hadn’t germinated. Deeper sowing has made a big difference here this year, shallower seeds were up quicker and more likely to survive. Keep an eye on crows, I’ve seen wheat in very wet patches due to springs emerge terribly slowly compared to the rest of the field. It was so slow that I thought it was gone. I took my eye off it and the crows attacked the patches as they eventually emerged. I can’t believe what I’ve seen wheat survive, I’m not exaggerating.
I know it’s off thread but what about oats in a similar situation ?

Probably in between barley and wheat. If a patch of barley gets waterlogged then you have a bare patch, then working out from the patch you have very poor barley, then poor barley and then normal barley. With a wet patch of wheat or oats, you’ll have  a bare patch and then normal plants. Also, thin wheat or oats can set extra grains per ear and tiller more. Barley can’t compensate to the same degree. Spring oats here got a fair beating with wet in March. I don’t think I’ve ever had such a high yielding crop of spring oats. Very close to 4tn/ac (virgin oat ground).
If it dried up I would stick in a second winter wheat. There is going to be a shortage of spring barley seed and I have have enough area destined for Beans .

How do you fare with the slatted boards v conventional? I'm seriously thinking of changing to slatted board, we have very brittle soil and you'd tear your hair out some years with soil sticking. You are doing very nice work to be fair to you.

I only went slats because a friend who I was buying 2 ploughs with,  (A 5F each) wanted slats. I don't think that they plough as nice a sod as No.28s, but they never stick, so if you are getting issues with sticking on No.8s slats are the answer. If I was buying again tomorrow I probably would go back to No.28s, but I would probably regret it a week later. Slats seem dearer to run than boards to me, but I haven't had them long enough to be sure by how much.
In what way do you think the slats are not as nice gone? Coming from 8's to the older one piece slat, I am not completely convinced either. More broken finish but possibly not as inverted as level.

It don't feel it works as well as No.28s at bigger widths and I think it doesn't invert the sod as well as No.28s, but I would think No30 slats are at least a match for No.8s at over 16". Just No.28s are in a class of their own in good ground.

Out of interest when your growing a trial plot of new varieties how on earth do put on fertilizer and crop protection products , I take it each individual plot just gets the same as in you fert and spray the same as you would for one complete field or do you have to do individual plots????
Would ye be brave enough

Ha, fair question. In the variety testing, all of the varieties that I am testing in a given trial will get the same chemical and fertiliser inputs at the same time. In general, each variety is planted 4 times (4 replications) and I will often give no fungicide to one replication of each variety to compare their genetic disease resistance. In the trial where I was using the thread, this is purely a wheat trial to test for Septoria resistance. The plots are very small and are only for disease observations, they won't be compared for yield. In this case, all of the plots will receive the normal level of inputs except for fungicides for which they will receive none. I hope that makes sense?
@Big Vern this is me spraying the variety plots today, as you can see the tramlines run at 90 degrees to the plots so they can all get the exact same treatment.

We had a wheat variety with similarly bad straw in trials, it yielded extremely well in official trials in 2022 but lodged in official trials in 2023. I decided to stop it progressing any further once I saw the first plot of it lodged, it hadn’t been harvested yet but there was no point fooling myself or anyone else. I’m testing varieties with 20 years, it’s a pet hate of mine when people market/sell varieties that haven’t been properly tested and have known serious weaknesses.

Yeah, the official trials showed a different picture, the lodging weakness was even mentioned by the Dept of Ag at the Teagasc crops seminar in September. In fairness, these kind of varieties can get away fine in some fields but they show up when pushed. I just hate the marketing that’s used despite the fact that such weaknesses can end up costing a grower a lot of money. If a variety had some special qualities like 10% more yield or was fabulous on disease then one could weigh up the risks but that doesn’t apply with this variety.

What do most people do for PGR on WW? Is CCC and Modus enough or would some people go with CCC twice and modus with the second round?

It really depends, Variety , some stand better than others. Sowing date, later sown will move more quickly so opportunity for PGR can pass more quickly. Rotation , second year after grass can be very fertile. Nitrogen input , organic manures etc. In a regular October sown crop after a break crop and assuming the variety isn’t too bad for lodging, I usually go with two applications of PGR. The first will be a mix of CCC& Moddus. If playing it very safe, it’ll be 1.5 - 1.8L of CCC plus 0.2 of Moddus. Today I pulled the Moddus back to 0.1L as I’m early and I’m wary of the cold night. Moddus is lethal but does its job. The second application will be at flag leaf peeping and is usually Medax Max although Terpal is good if the straw isn’t going for mushrooms. PGR’s really are an input that needs experience, I’ve done scorching in the past with them and I’m sure I’ll do it again. I’m sure that others will have different approaches.

I have a lot of random plants scattered through the field much more advanced than the main crop. Thought there was an infestation of wild oats early on but appears to be maybe a different variety of seed? Dawsum was the variety. Does Dawsum have awns?
It is in a lot of the Dawsum. I think @Crops explained what it was last year.

Yep, it’s a type of genetic off type that occurs in Dawsum. It’s been in the variety from Day 1. I’ve seen this kind of thing in winter and spring wheat varieties in the past. It doesn’t always happen and my opinion is that stress (water logging, compaction etc) early in the crops life seems to encourage it. In Dawsum, you could actually have some off type tillers and some normal tillers on the same plant including a tiller with awns. It can look a bit messy at this stage but the crop evens up as the weeks pass.

Interesting that you have it worse in one field than the other - that matches my observations about some crops having more of it than others despite all crops having seed of the same original origin. We used to get it a lot in some spring wheat varieties in the past with stress making it show worse in some fields. I still remember two particular cases: One was where the farmer had sprayed his crop with a 2wd tractor. The front wheels had gone across the headland tramline a bit each time he came to the end of a run, I guess he was making sure that he didn't miss any bits. The taller tillers appeared where the front wheels had driven into the crop. In another case, a crop of spring wheat had the talls showing in patches scattered across the field. The farmer eventually copped that the talls were showing where bales of straw had sat the previous winter. In the case of the bales, you could say that it was due to some nutrients leaching from the straw bales (like Potassium).

Interesting, I have three nine acre fields planted with Dawsum, two of them may have a dozen plants showing well above the rest. The third field would have at least hundreds or more plants. Was convinced it was oats. All three fields adjacent to one another and identical soil. As always, the green variety will need to yield etc. The straw strength looks good in it. Also, time will tell if the Septoria resistance is robust. Many varieties (Cellule was an example) have started off their life very clean by relying on one resistance gene but the Septoria soon overcame them. Others have remained very strong against the disease.
The interest for me in trial plots is always the untreated ones. They tell a story. Super illustration of good trial work there. Bravo........... or folpet or whatever.

Winter wheat has a lot of people worried this year. Full rates of expensive chemicals giving poor to moderate levels of Septoria control at best. I have grown to really like and respect winter wheat as a crop but I can see many growers will look at winter barley in a first cereal slot as a replacement if they have land suitable for winter barley. As mentioned, 3 separate winter wheat breeders visited in the few days. All from well known UK breeding companies but with activities throughout the UK and Europe. Septoria resistance is now the main target for these big companies but it’s not an easy one to solve. For years, the chemistry was so effective that genetic resistance wasn’t as important in the market but that has changed and they have catching up to do. In addition, the recent wet springs in the UK, France etc have made Septoria a big issue in regions that used not have the same pressure. One of the breeders said that Northern French growers are really struggling with it. These increased efforts should benefit Ireland in the long run. I’ve mentioned before that we (Goldcrop) have really increased our focus on finding wheats with better Septoria resistance. For many years, the breeders mentioned above used to just send perhaps 8-10 varieties each to us for testing each year. So, let’s say 30 varieties each year. These would be varieties that were a year ahead in the UK and had initially been selected over there. These would be successful breeders. Now, over the past 3-4 years, they have started sending us the above varieties but also now potential varieties from earlier stages in the breeding program. Some of the material being sent is bred to become parents of new varieties, so really early material. The total number being tested in our trials from these 3 breeders alone has increased to approximately 2000 per year, all with the aim of spotting Septoria resistance in a climate where they will be well tested. One of the breeders in particular can see this new trialing making a big difference in his breeding program since he first started with the large scale testing here 4 years ago. Here is a picture of this years Septoria plots, all 2000 of them. I put in barley plots to aid with navigating the plots. All untreated for disease, only used for Septoria data at this stage, not harvested for yield measurement.

Is take-all a risk in the crop following winter wheat always? Could it affect winter barley? I’m planning on ordering WB seed next week. Just need to know if I should add Lattitude.

Take-All effects both winter barley and winter wheat. It generally effects wheat more severely but it also takes yield from winter barley. Rye can also get Take-All despite what some people say. Warm soils (early sowing), fluffy seedbeds, grass weeds like Scutch all encourage the disease. Lime just before planting can also enourage Take-All. Take-All is worse in years 2 to 5 (if Year 1 is after a break crop). After year 5 it generally tapers off a bit as other fungi in the soil build up and compete with the Take-All fungus, this is known as Take-All decline. However, in certain years, even after Take-All decline, the disease can flare up and hit crops hard. I remember the last real flare up in Take-All was 2007. I generally won't plant winter barley now unless it is after a break crop, not a common approach I know but I have seen the difference in recent years. Latitude is questionable on winter barley in terms of economic return but is a no brainer on wheat. Most people don't bother with it on winter barley but some do.

Don't want to tempt fate but, I have been at continuous WB on a block of 30 acres for the past 5 years and never used Latitude and have hit either 4 or more ton/acre every year off that block. Have been hit with BYDV and waterlogging in other parts that hurt yield but never noticed take all as being an issue. Sowing date always early October. Mostly it's hybrid barley i am using. Would variety or soil type have a bearing?

I have never seen variety to have an effect but soil type will have an effect - heavier soils being less effected. Sources of N like organic manures etc will also help to keep the plant going even if the roots are somewhat compromised by the disease.

Found this on Bayer website, so weather also has a bearing: Autumn sowings are most at risk, especially in cold, wet seasons and in marginal soils with poor drainage and fertility. In good soils other micro-organisms and better root growth help reduce the damage caused by this disease. Optimum conditions for disease are a mild winter, a moist cool spring and a dry early summer. Light, alkaline soils can also be favourable for this disease ....... I use pig slurry ahead of sowing and this block of land is free draining with a good slope off it so no water logging, so I guess that helped. One last one on the take all, see off the uk farming forum they reckon there is little or no incidence of take all in no till where there is competition from other soil fungi. Can’t comment on the no-till/other soil fungi thing as I don’t know. There is a possibility that you are removing the host of the Take-All (the cereal roots) and introducing a plant that it doesn’t like (the cover crop) but it will only be there for a short while. I’d imagine that your pig slurry is a big factor in your favour. What has pig slurry got in abundance? Phosphorus. What do roots love? Phosphorus. What has pig slurry got in abundance? Nitrogen. What helps a cereal crop get over some of the effects of Take All? Nitrogen…

Fitzroy should be added to the Recommended List here this autumn. Has yielded reliably in official trials. Is very good on Septoria, stands well and is slow developing. Kph is a bit lower than I’d like but so are other varieties.

We had a field that got take all and as you approached the gate there was less take all because of historical compaction. A local farmer used to flat roll winter wheat in the spring . Also if you use Ammonium Sulphate as the nitrogen source. Unless there is some reason I would go for a break crop as modern Prima donna varieties  do not like running on bald tyres .

Worked out average winter wheat yield here, adjusted to 20%mc it came in at 3.9tn/ac. The lowest average we ever had I think. A roasting from Septoria was the main cause, it was almost unstoppable. The majority was later sown (end of November) and it was a bit thinner than I would like for a late sown crop. Most of the wheat was inland and got a rub of that very heavy rain that hurt spring barley crops. Normally that location would have slightly lower Septoria pressure than the coast but it was the other way around this year, I think that heavy rain really drove the Septoria mad - it was the worst I ever saw. Straw was chopped and it was mostly for seed so there will still be a reasonably good margin. We will stick with winter wheat as it has been very good to us and is good for the tougher ground. New varieties coming for next autumn and hopefully new chemistry for 2026.
